hints: There are several ways to approach this. You might think of how each variable is likely to affect the pressure, or you might use the numerical values and units of the given quantities to try to match to the best fit line you drew in the previous part.
problem_text:
[{"motor_ref":"bea0e406","motor_type":"DragDropMathjax","motor_json":"{\"problemText\":\"The student decided to use their results to make a linear model for the pressure under water.\\n\\nThe variables they came up with to put into an equation for the pressure at a given depth under the water were\\n\\\\begin{tabular}{l | c | r}\\n\\\\text{quantity} & \\\\text{variable} & \\\\text{value} \\\\\\\\\\n\\\\hline\\n\\\\text{ pressure in the water} & $P$\\\\, & \\\\text{varies} \\\\\\\\\\n\\\\text{ density of the water} & $\\\\rho_{\\\\rm water}$ & $10^3 \\\\;\\\\mathrm{kg\\/m^3}$ \\\\\\\\\\n\\\\text{ density of air} & $\\\\rho_{\\\\rm air}$ & $1.2 \\\\;\\\\mathrm{kg\\/m^3}$ \\\\\\\\\\n\\\\text{ gravitational acceleration} & $g$ & $9.8 \\\\;\\\\mathrm{m\\/s^2}$ \\\\\\\\\\n\\\\text{ atmospheric pressure} & $P_0$ & $1.01 \\\\times 10^5 \\\\;\\\\mathrm{Pa}$ \\\\\\\\\\n\\\\text{ depth under the water surface} & d\\\\, & \\\\text{varies}\\n\\\\end{tabular}\\n\\nWhat equation made from these variables best models the data?\\n\\nYou do not need to use every variable.\\n\",\"type\":\"DragDropMathjax\",\"parseFormat\":\"draggableInEachDropArea\",\"outputDisplayFormat\":\"contentInEachDropArea\",\"options\":{\"draggableContents\":[\"$g$\",\"$d$\",\"$P_0$\",\"$\\\\rho_{\\\\rm water}$\",\"$\\\\rho_{\\\\rm air}$\"],\"mathjax\":\"\\\\[P = $\\\\dragdrop{????????}$ + $\\\\dragdrop{????????}$$\\\\dragdrop{????????}$$\\\\dragdrop{????????}$. \\\\]\"},\"problem_text_fmt\":\"The student decided to use their results to make a linear model for the pressure under water.
\\n
\\nThe variables they came up with to put into an equation for the pressure at a given depth under the water were
\\n
\\nWhat equation made from these variables best models the data?
\\n
\\nYou do not need to use every variable.
\\n\"}"}]
solution_text:
We see from the answer format that we will have one term, a plus sign, and another term. This is a good fit for the equation of a line in slope-intercept form, which in math class we know as $y = mx + b.$

There is some pressure in the water due to the pressure of the atmosphere pushing down on it. This is the pressure right at $0$ depth, and the variable for it is $P_0,$ so we have
$$P = P_0 + \Delta P.$$

This makes great sense given our graph, which had an intercept on the vertical axis right at $101 \;\mathrm{kPa},$ which is equal to $P_0.$

The $\Delta P$ is the "change" - what causes the pressure to increase as we go to greater depth. This will certainly involve the depth term, $d.$ It needs to by multiplied to the slope of our line.

Intuitively, the density of the water should matter. If water has a lot of mass, that should create a lot of pressure. Gravitational acceleration should also matter. The higher gravity, the more the water weighs, and the greater the pressure.

Let's look at $ho_{m water} g.$ That comes to
$$ egin{aligned}
ho_{m water} \cdot g &= 10^3 \dfrac{\mathrm{kg}}{\mathrm{m}^3} \cdot 9.8 \;\mathrm{m/s^2} \[6pt]
  &= 9.8 	imes 10^3 \dfrac{\mathrm{kg}}{\mathrm{m^2 s^2}}.
\end{aligned}$$

This is essentially the same as the slope we calculated in the previous part of the problem, so we conclude 
$$ oxed{P = P_0 + ho_{m water} g d}.$$
